original_abstract:
This paper describes the ECCO version 4 (ECCO v4) non-linear inverse modeling framework and its foundational global ocean state estimate for the period 1992–2011. The framework represents a significant evolution in the ECCO project, integrating a global model configuration, an automated regression testing suite, and a specialized Matlab toolbox for grid-independent analysis. Key architectural advancements include the adoption of the Lat-Lon-Cap (LLC) global grid, which eliminates the North Pole singularity and incorporates the Arctic Ocean, and the implementation of a non-linear free surface with real freshwater flux. These features ensure exact local and global conservation of volume and tracers, providing a more physically consistent dynamical interpolation of observations.

The baseline ECCO v4 solution achieves a significantly improved fit to in situ hydrography—particularly Argo profiles—and satellite altimetry compared to previous generations of the model. Through the use of algorithmic differentiation, the framework enables the simultaneous adjustment of atmospheric forcing, initial conditions, and internal mixing parameters. A key finding of this study is the critical role of parameterized physics in state estimation: the inversion of spatially varying turbulent transport coefficients (such as Gent–McWilliams intensity and isopycnal diffusivity) was essential for reducing model-data misfits and mitigating long-term drifts. Preliminary assessments suggest that parametric and external forcing uncertainties are of comparable magnitude and generally outweigh structural model errors. The complete ECCO v4 configuration, including the adjusted controls and the diagnostic tools required to reproduce or extend the estimate, is made publicly available to facilitate community-wide ocean and climate research.
humanized_abstract:
We present the ECCO version 4 (ECCO v4) non-linear inverse modeling framework, and the global ocean state estimate it provides for the period 1992–2011. This model represents a major advance over previous versions in several aspects: first, it includes a global model configuration with an associated set of experimental tools including an automated regression testing framework, and a Matlab toolbox for a range of grid-independent diagnostics. Second, the model rests on a Lat-Lon-Cap (LLC) global grid, which eliminates the North Pole singularity in vertical coordinate transformations and includes the Arctic Ocean. Third, it uses a non-linear free surface with real freshwater fluxes, ensuring that the interpolated dynamics conserve volume and tracers exactly in all local and global averaging senses.

The baseline ECCO v4 solution has greatly improved fit to in situ hydrography, in particular Argo data, as well as to satellite altimetry, when compared to the earlier versions of the model. The framework as a whole makes use of algorithmic differentiation to allow simultaneous adjustments to the atmospheric forcing, initial conditions, and the internal mixing parameters. An important result of the study is that the parameterized physics terms play a crucial role in the state estimation problem. In particular, allowing the spatial variation of the turbulent transport coefficients, e.g. Gent-McWilliams intensity, as well as isopycnal diffusivity, to be inverted proved crucial in reducing the residuals of the model to the data and also in reducing the large trend that arises otherwise. Initial estimates of the impact of parametric and external forcing uncertainty suggest that the amplitude of these uncertainties are comparable to one another, and are generally larger than those associated with structural errors within the model itself. The ECCO v4 solution with all of the necessary parameters for the adjusted model controls, as well as the components needed for reproducing and extending the estimated state, is provided.

document.output:
